provisions.title: G20 Framework for Strong, Sustainable and Balanced Growth
provisions.text: At the Pittsburgh Summit in September 2009, G20 leaders launched the Framework for Strong, Sustainable and Balanced Growth. The framework established a mutual assessment process (MAP) coordinated by the IMF. Key commitments for financial regulation include: all standardised OTC derivatives contracts should be traded on exchanges or electronic platforms and cleared through central counterparties by end-2012; and non-centrally cleared contracts should be subject to higher capital requirements. The summit also agreed on compensation reforms to align pay with long-term value creation.

provisions.title: G20 Global LEI Initiative
provisions.text: At the Cannes Summit in 2011, G20 leaders endorsed the FSB recommendation to establish a global Legal Entity Identifier (LEI) system. The LEI would provide a unique identification code for all legal entities participating in financial transactions worldwide. The system would be governed by a Regulatory Oversight Committee (ROC) comprising financial regulators, with operational management by a central operating unit (later established as the Global LEI Foundation, GLEIF). The LEI initiative aims to improve risk management, transparency, and market integrity.

provisions.title: TLAC Standard Endorsement
provisions.text: At the Antalya Summit in November 2015, G20 leaders endorsed the FSB's Total Loss-Absorbing Capacity (TLAC) standard for global systemically important banks (G-SIBs). The TLAC standard requires G-SIBs to maintain minimum TLAC of 16% of RWA from 1 January 2019, rising to 18% from 1 January 2022. In addition, TLAC must be at least 6% of the Basel III leverage ratio exposure measure from 2019, rising to 6.75% from 2022. TLAC instruments must be subordinated to excluded liabilities.

provisions.title: Crypto-Asset Risk Monitoring
provisions.text: At the Buenos Aires Summit in 2018, G20 leaders called for further monitoring of crypto-assets and their risks. The communique noted that crypto-assets raise issues with respect to consumer and investor protection, market integrity, tax evasion, money laundering, and terrorist financing. Leaders asked the FSB, FATF, and other standard-setting bodies to continue their monitoring and assessment of risks and to report back with any needed policy responses. Leaders also noted that technological innovations, including distributed ledger technology, could deliver significant benefits to the financial system and the broader economy.

provisions.title: G20 Crypto-Asset Framework Endorsement
provisions.text: At the New Delhi Summit in 2023, G20 leaders endorsed the FSB's global regulatory framework for crypto-asset activities and stablecoin arrangements. The endorsement covers: the FSB's high-level recommendations for crypto-asset activities (same activity, same risk, same regulation); the FSB-IOSCO joint recommendations for crypto and digital asset markets; and the IMF-FSB Synthesis Paper on crypto-asset policies. Leaders called for swift implementation across jurisdictions.

provisions.title: G20 Under South Africa: Inclusive Growth and Financial Resilience
provisions.text: The G20 under South Africa's presidency (2025) prioritised solidarity, equality, and sustainability. Key financial regulation themes include: strengthening financial resilience against climate-related shocks; promoting financial inclusion in Africa and the developing world; addressing digital public infrastructure for financial services; monitoring implementation of the crypto-asset regulatory framework; and assessing the financial stability implications of AI in the financial sector. The presidency also focused on the reform of international financial institutions.

provisions.title: CPMI-IOSCO Assessment Methodology for PFMI
provisions.text: CPMI-IOSCO has developed an assessment methodology for evaluating the implementation of the Principles for Financial Market Infrastructures. The assessment covers three levels: Level 1 (whether jurisdictions have adopted legislation and regulations to implement the PFMI); Level 2 (the completeness and consistency of the implementing measures with the PFMI); and Level 3 (the consistency of outcomes achieved by FMIs with the PFMI). Assessments are conducted periodically and results are published to promote transparency and peer accountability.

provisions.title: CPMI-IOSCO Public Quantitative Disclosure Standards for CCPs
provisions.text: CPMI-IOSCO requires CCPs to make public quantitative disclosures on a quarterly basis. The disclosures cover: total initial margin required and held; total default fund contributions required and held; the results of back-testing of margin models (the number and size of margin breaches); the results of stress testing (the CCP's cover-2 requirement and any uncovered losses); liquidity resources and stress test results; and operational statistics (transaction volumes, settlement values, number of participants). These disclosures promote transparency and enable clearing members and their clients to assess CCP risk management practices.

provisions.title: CPMI Analysis of Repo Market Resilience
provisions.text: CPMI-IOSCO has analysed the functioning of repo markets during stress episodes. Key findings include: repo markets are critical for funding and liquidity management but can amplify stress through margin spirals and collateral hoarding; central clearing of repos can reduce bilateral counterparty credit risk but creates concentration at CCPs; haircut floors on non-centrally cleared securities financing transactions (SFTs) can reduce procyclicality; and transparency of repo market activity is essential for monitoring financial stability risks. The FSB has established a framework for minimum haircut standards on SFTs.

provisions.title: Critical Service Providers to FMIs
provisions.text: CPMI-IOSCO has published expectations for critical service providers (CSPs) to financial market infrastructures. A CSP is a third-party service provider whose disruption could materially affect an FMI's ability to perform its critical operations. Key expectations include: FMIs should identify and manage risks from CSPs; contractual arrangements should address service levels, audit rights, and incident reporting; CSPs should meet comparable operational risk management standards to the FMIs they serve; FMIs should have contingency plans for CSP failure; and supervisors should have appropriate oversight arrangements for CSPs that serve multiple FMIs.

provisions.title: Reducing FX Settlement Risk
provisions.text: CPMI has published guidance on reducing foreign exchange settlement risk (also known as Herstatt risk). Key measures include: payment-versus-payment (PvP) settlement through CLS Bank and other PvP mechanisms; bilateral netting to reduce gross settlement obligations; the use of central counterparties for FX derivatives; exposure management (intraday monitoring of FX settlement exposures); and operational improvements to compress the settlement window. CPMI has expressed concern that the share of FX transactions settled without PvP protection has increased, particularly in emerging market currencies.

provisions.title: CPMI Framework for Fast Payments
provisions.text: CPMI has published analytical frameworks for fast (instant) payment systems. Key design considerations include: availability (24/7/365 operation); speed (end-to-end processing within seconds); irrevocability (finality of payment upon crediting beneficiary's account); open access (interoperability across payment service providers); risk management (real-time fraud detection, AML/CFT controls, liquidity management); and overlay services (request-to-pay, QR code payments, cross-border interoperability). CPMI notes that fast payment systems should be designed to support future innovation while maintaining safety and efficiency.

national_mappings.national_framework: FSA Japan Banking Capital Adequacy Guidelines
national_mappings.requirement_summary: Japan has implemented Basel III capital requirements through the JFSA's Banking Capital Adequacy Guidelines. CET1 minimum is 4.5%, with domestic SIB surcharges for major banks.

APRA has implemented Basel III capital requirements through APS 110 (Capital Adequacy), APS 111 (Capital Adequacy Measurement of Capital), and APS 112 (Capital Adequacy Standardised Approach to Credit Risk). CET1 minimum is 4.5%, with a D-SIB buffer of 1% for major banks.

national_mappings.national_reference: MAS Notice 637 on Risk Based Capital Adequacy Requirements
national_mappings.requirement_summary: MAS has implemented Basel III capital requirements through Notice 637. D-SIB banks are subject to a HLA requirement of 2%. MAS has also implemented Basel III leverage ratio and liquidity requirements.

Brazil has implemented CDD requirements through BCB Circular 3978, covering identification and verification of customers and beneficial owners, ongoing monitoring, and risk-based approach. The circular also covers simplified and enhanced CDD measures.
national_mappings.gap_notes: FATF mutual evaluation noted need for improved beneficial ownership verification for legal persons.
